speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number three. The rescue and aid givers name is Renee Scott.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were telling us about your first day at Gestapo. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That was with my kids. [BACKGROUND NOISE] That was all till about- was is 2- till about 3 o'clock in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time did you arrive at the Gestapo office?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 4 o'clock in the afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they start questioning you right away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, describe the room that they took you to.

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the room that they took you into for the questioning that day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well he asked if I knew this one, if I'd been here, if I'd been there, what I had done here, what time I was there, what days I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they seem to have accurate information about where you had been?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, they did. Yeah. They know even the restaurants in Paris where I went with Simone.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the months before your arrest--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you have a feeling that you were being followed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I told you that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me tell me about that. Tell me about those--

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you. I was in the train and that individual next to me wanted to help me with my coat and when I got off the train, I felt that he was spying on me. And John, his nephew used to come and meet me at the train. And I had a feeling. I had told him we must hurry up and get out of here. There's something wrong. But I didn't want to tell them why. But I said I had a man next to me. I didn't like him. He said, well, why? What'd he do? I said, he didn't do anything, but I don't like him. So I said, I don't want him to know where I live.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he always came with flowers to meet, John's nephew, to take me home. So he said that's OK. He said we'll take a cab. So there were so many people, the station was crowded, so we skipped him. He lost us and we got back to my house. But it's only a few days after that that they came and arrested me. So you were in Paris before you took the train to go to Brussels.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you arrived in Brussels and you were on vacation for a few days and when you returned from vacation was when they arrested you? OK. So before that, before the man on the train--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: --back in Paris--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you feel that you were being followed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in Paris, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the first time you felt that way was on the train to Brussels?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was always from Brussels-- from Paris to Brussels. In Paris by itself, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't think you would have been arrested in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well if-- they didn't have probably enough information at that time. They wanted to get more, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went up to Brussels that time before you got arrested, were you involved in any underground activity before you went on vacation that weekend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was still working with-- I had brought the things.

speaker: interviewer
text: That particular time, right before you were arrested, what was it that you brought?

speaker: interviewee
text: I brought-- I can't remember how many I brought. I brought documents and things, but I know that each time I went, I brought something-- some papers or, you know, like yellow envelope, or I had-- what do you call it? I have it here-- briefcase, a briefcase.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember specifically who you met that time before you got arrested?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who I met?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who you met, who you transferred the papers to in the underground.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah it was almost at the same place, at the unknown soldier, an office in the building by itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Always at the same place? And always the same person?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have an idea that they were also involved. I never knew it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you arrived at the building by the unknown soldier, tell me what would happen. How would you deliver the papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Took the elevator, went up to the office. I said I had something from Paris to give them. I gave it to them from the Chamber of Commerce and that was it. I didn't know if-- I didn't know at that time if they were involved, but after, I always thought that they were because they never questioned me or anything. But Simone, of course, was always in touch with them. She knew where I was going.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said Simone was arrested before you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not long, maybe two or three days before me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those papers that you delivered, they were not from Simone?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were from Simone, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: She had given you those papers before she was arrested?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: After she was arrested, weren't you worried that they knew about you?

speaker: interviewee
text: After she was arrested, I was arrested only a few days after her. I didn't-- my-- the owner of the building, when she told me the Gestapo was there, one time before that-- I skipped this, I'm sorry-- my head's getting a little tired. A few days before the Gestapo came to arrest me, Simone had made a special trip to come and tell me, warn me of something. I never knew what. And my landlady told me that the young girl had come from Paris to speak to me, but she had said she had no time to stay. That was Simone. But I never could get in touch with her. We couldn't telephone anymore from Brussels to Paris. Everything was cut. So I never knew what she wanted to tell me. And a few days after, that she was arrested. And a few days after she was, I was arrested.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out Simone was arrested?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Gestapo told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't know she was arrested until the Gestapo told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Baganz told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He told me that my concubine, my-- how would I say-- my right hand had been arrested. So he said, you know her, don't you? I said, no, I don't know her. I always denied everything. I said I didn't even know her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even though you worked with her, you said you didn't know her.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said I didn't know. I always said I knew nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Simone ever prepare you for what happened when you were arrested? Did she ever talk to you about what to do and what not to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only on the train when we went to Germany together, when the Gestapo came and knocked on the-- you know, we were in the old wooden trains and there was a bunch of other prisoners like us in the train. And he came and knocked and he said, [SPEAKING GERMAN], what's your name? So I told him. He said, well, komm mit, come with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went with him and here I was in front of Simone. Poor Simone. And she took me in her arms and she cried and she cried and she cried and she said, this is what I did to you. Excuse me. [CRYING] Excuse me. And I asked her, I said, did you hear from my mother? Or did you hear from my family? Pardon me. She said, no. But she said, my sister did. Everybody's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they didn't know where I was. They didn't know anything. She didn't say anything. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was the end, when they-- I saw Baganz. I never got in touch with anybody. The wall was closing. [CRYING] Sorry. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's go back to when you were first arrested by the Gestapo. That first day they- that first day that the Gestapo arrested you in Brussels, they interrogated you for hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hours and hours, yes. Then when they finished questioning me, they took me to the prison and the number of my cell was 151. No, pardon me, 153. The same number as my ride here. I take the ride to go to BAB. That's the number, my number. Anyway, 153 and I'll always remember. And I was in the cell and they through me in that cell. There was a mattress, a dirty mattress on the floor. And I sat there with my Deanna Durbin hat and my umbrella in my hand and I think in the early morning, I was still sitting there, the same way. And I heard a big key in the door, you know, in prison door. And they were bringing-- they asked me, [SPEAKING GERMAN], what's your name and this and that. And they gave me, they threw in a cup of supposedly coffee or something. I was so thirsty, I drank it. It tasted like camphor. They put camphor in the coffee. They do that with the military too, you know. So anyway, it was horrible, but I drank it anyway. And they left me like that, just sitting there, till about, I don't know, probably early afternoon. I had no time or nothing. I couldn't see anything. The window was-- there was one window, way up high in the cell. And then in the afternoon, they came and got me and they took me downstairs in the prison. And they took all the information they wanted about me, my name, and this and that. Took all my jewelry off, which I never saw again, of course. And then they put me in another cell, in another place. It was 154. 154, next door. And there, I was in there and I just had a bed and a little thing with water where you could get washed up and a pail. In the afternoon, I heard knocking on the pipes. That was a code the girls gave. The prisoners would talk like that, my code. So I had to learn, if I could, what they wanted to say. Then one time-- I got-- I was used to the prison after seven months of a solitary confinement, so I would push my bed-- on the bed, you'd fold it up like a bunk bed and you'd put a board on top. That was your table. But I had nothing to read. I had nothing to-- I couldn't write. I couldn't read, nothing, I had nothing. So I pushed that against the wall and I still was vary able with my legs. I could climb and I climbed up and I could see them outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had monks. There were some monks that were arrested, poor monks. And they had great big chains. They were all chained together and they would run like that. That was intriguing me, which I said, what do I hear like that, like chains all the time? And that way I saw what was going on. And there were great big vegetable gardens there. The prisoners had to make those gardens. And that's where they used to go for-- once a day, they'd go for 15 minutes to get some fresh air. Did Baganz ever interrogate you again after that first night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Baganz? After that night? Once again, yes, once, once. Never again after that. He said he had everything he needed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I always told him that he didn't have anything because I didn't say-- I never admitted anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never-- you never gave them any information that first day?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well he had everything down in Berlin. He sent everything to Berlin. When I got to Berlin, after we got off the train, we were all chained, we had handcuffs on, all handcuffed to Gestapo guy, a guard. And they took us to a great big-- at Alexanderplatz. And there we were all put in one great big room, like you'd say this apartment, you know. And there, Simone was there with me and all many other girls. And that's where they took Simone away. I told you, they came and told her that she had to go with them to take baths. And we thought, oh, that's good. We're tired. We need a bath. And she never came back. They decapitated her. They beheaded her right away. Of course she had suffered so much in Paris that they knew what they were going to do to her right away. They knew it. She wasn't going to live two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Scott, let's go back to Brussels again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it that you were kept for those seven months in Brussels? At St. Gilles, the big prison of St. Gilles, right near where I lived in Brussels. What was the food like the seven months that you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food? Don't talk about food. Rutabaga, turnip, turnip-- three and a half years of turnip in prison and in camps. One piece of bread a day. At the prison, we had that horrible coffee that we drink anyway. And in the camp, it was the same thing. In the camp, we had-- you know what you give to little birds, those little seeds-- we had that once a week and we thought that was so good. They put [? anisette ?] and something sweet in it and we thought it was good. But that's what we had-- one piece of bread today. Once a month, we had a half a piece of a sausage. We didn't know if it was made with human beings or what. It was horrible. But we ate it. We were hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned at St. Gilles, you were able to communicate with the other girls through the tapping.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of code did you use?

speaker: interviewee
text: We made them up. We'd tell each other if we knocked two times, it means yes. If we knocked four times, it means no. Then we were caught several times too. I remember one time I was sitting on the floor and I was so busy knocking and giving my code and the key in the door. And here he was, the Gestapo there. Said, [SPEAKING GERMAN], names, nice names. What are you doing there?

speaker: interviewer
text: So he picked me up by the collar. He gave me two whacks like that and he said don't begin that again because you'll go in the chamber downstairs. So I was careful after that because I said I don't want to get more than that, you know? But I used to always climb at the window and look. You mentioned the chamber downstairs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a black chamber. They put you in a black cell and they just leave you there, nothing to eat, nothing to drink, rats and everything else. You just stay there. Water running down, you know, just an old corner of a cellar. They call it the black chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go down there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only-- as I said, when Baganz interrogated me, the first time, that first night, I had to wait until they came to get me to bring me to prison and they put me in a place like that, but not long.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned Baganz interrogated you a second time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, twice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the second time and when did it happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: About a week after I was arrested. And I had to go back and I said, oh, no, please, I don't want to go back and be beaten up. But they didn't beat me that time. He just told me that he had-- he had had all the information he wanted on me and that I was no good, that I was a traitor and I was a no good and that I would go and die in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did Baganz look like? Describe his face to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I showed you his picture-- ugly, a great, big, strong, Nazi guy, bad. See them on the film? Did you see that Nazi on the film? Exactly him. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Cruel, very cruel-- they have cruel faces. They're cruel people. Don't you think so? That first night in prison, did you regret your work with the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I do?

speaker: interviewer
text: That very first night in prison--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you regret the work you had done with Simone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. What could I regret? I was there? No. I couldn't do anything. I was crying for my family because I said what are they going to think? Where are they going to find me? What's going to happen to them and my daughter? But I didn't regret. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I always-- it's silly, but I always had, after that, I always had one little hope that maybe I would get out of prison. It was silly to think that because I would never get out there, but I thought that. Sometimes you have hope, you know, and certain things that make you think you can have-- I'd look at the blue sky and I'd say, oh, if only I could get out and go have a walk in the nice sun and go back home. But I didn't regret what I had done. I did it, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned that there was a week between when Baganz first interrogated you and a second time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that week. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in prison. Nothing happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: They never questioned you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They just left you there. Nothing to do, nothing to read, nothing to look at.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do to pass the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exercise.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd dance. [LAUGHS] I'd dance and I'd exercise, and-- I wouldn't sing, but I'd exercise a lot and dance. What could I do? I couldn't even sleep. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't do anything. Did the Nazi guards ever see you dancing in the cell?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] They didn't care about that as long as you didn't interfere with looking too much by the window or something. They didn't care.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the next time you were questioned after Baganz questioned you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Alexanderplatz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So during the entire seven months that you were in St. Gilles, were only questioned twice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Then a third time in Alexanderplatz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me a little bit more about the seven months that you were in St. Gilles.

speaker: interviewee
text: About what?

speaker: interviewer
text: About the remainder of the time that you were in St. Gilles, after that second interrogation. What did you do for seven months?

speaker: interviewee
text: In prison?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I couldn't do anything. I can't tell you anything. I don't know. I didn't do anything, just danced and exercised.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to get any information about the war or your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not there. Nope. I tried to get in touch-- somebody had told me-- tap-- that John was arrested. So-- this, I forgot tell you. They told me if you go to the dentist, you want to have a tooth pulled. I said I don't want to have a tooth pulled. But they said if you have an excuse, tell them that you have a toothache, you go to the dentist and maybe you'll see if John is there because you'll probably see some men around. So I went to the dentist, but John was not arrested. I didn't see any man that I knew, anything. But they pulled a tooth out. [LAUGHS] I had to have an excuse. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That was crazy life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was not an exciting life. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It was rather monotonous-- days and days and days like that, nothing to do. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Think and pray, all you could do. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Once, maybe three times a week, they'd take me down to have fresh air in a-- where, I told you, they were growing vegetables in big gardens. And we had like little separate gardens with-- uh, how do you say that-- fences, big fences, like the-- like prison fence, you know. And we could walk there. There was a little flower bend in the middle. You could walk around it that way and then you turn around and walk that way. That would last about 20 minutes and then they'd get us back in our cell. That's all we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were able to maintain your strengthen and your health with your dancing and your exercising?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp- in the- yes I. One time I was not feeling too well and that was all excuses. I thought I didn't feel to good and I wanted to see the doctor. So when you want to see a doctor, they'd give you a little piece of paper and a pencil and you have to write arzt, doctor. So I did. And I went downstairs and I had a very big surprise there. The doctor was a charming elderly man and I had the habit always to put my feet like this, like a ballerina. And I had the ways in my hands, I don't know what.

words.text: In the camp- in the- yes I.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he looked at me and he sent his nurse out a minute and he said to me, in French, he said, were you a dancer? I said, why? I was afraid what he wanted to ask me. He said, I see the way you put your feet and your hands. He said, I'm a doctor, but I'm also a conductor at the opera of Berlin. He was at the opera at Berlin. And he said, why are you here? And he had like tears in his eyes. He said, why are you here? Why? Why? He said, I feel so bad for you. He said-- the nurse was going to come back, so he said, you come and see me again. So he ordered-- he made a prescription and he ordered me some hot water. I was so glad because I had no hot water. So every day I had, for a certain time, they'd bring me up some hot water in my cell. [LAUGHS] That's the only thing I got. [LAUGHS] But I got some hot water, at least wash myself. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm telling you. Now I laugh. I wasn't laughing. It was a funny life. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] He- he was arrested, this poor doctor. They- they arrested him because they found out he was too good with the prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you suffer any injuries after that first night when they were interrogating you? You said that they were beating you on the back of the head.

speaker: interviewee
text: My head, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you suffer any injuries as a result of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well headaches, headaches, terrible headaches. But then after a while, it passed. But then apparently, from what the doctors saw later on, that I had bled in back of my eyes and that's why now I've lost my sight.

speaker: interviewer
text: So approximately how long-- approximately how long were you at St. Gilles for?

speaker: interviewee
text: My what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately how long were you in the prison at St. Gilles for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went Alexanderplatz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the journey from St. Gilles to Alexanderplatz.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us down in the main lobby of the prison. We were all-- people I didn't know, prisoners, like me, but I didn't know them. They worked for them other-- r they didn't work for Simone. They worked I don't know with who. I didn't know these people. But we all went in the same train together. And they asked us if we had jewelry, if we had things like, you know, to take from the safe. I said, yes, I have jewelry but I haven't got it back. They didn't give it back to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, we waited there and we waited there and we waited there. And then they put us in police cars and they took us to the station. We all got in the train and towards evening, the train took off. And I think we took about three days to get to Alexanderplatz in Berlin. When we got there-- enough? Finish what you were saying. OK. We're going to finish the tape now. We're going to change tapes now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
